title: Maha Shivratri
intro: Maha Shivratri is one of the most sacred and significant Hindu festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva.
description:
Maha Shivratri is one of the most sacred and significant Hindu festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva. The festival is observed on the Chaturdashi (fourteenth day) of the Krishna Paksha in the Hindu month of Phalguna, usually falling in February or March. On this auspicious night, devotees observe fasts, perform Shiva Puja, chant “Om Namah Shivaya,” and stay awake throughout the night in devotion to Lord Shiva.

According to Hindu scriptures, Maha Shivratri is associated with several divine events, including the sacred marriage of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati, the appearance of the infinite Shiva Linga (Lingodbhava), and the night when Lord Shiva performed the cosmic Tandava dance. The festival symbolizes spiritual awakening, self-discipline, and the victory of knowledge over ignorance.

Temples across India are beautifully decorated, and devotees offer milk, water, honey, Bel Patra, Datura, and fruits to the Shiva Linga. Continuous chanting of Shiva Mantras, Rudrabhishek, recitation of the Shiva Purana, and night-long prayers are performed to seek Lord Shiva’s blessings for peace, prosperity, good health, and liberation (Moksha).

Festival Month

Phalguna (February – March)

Significance / Importance

Maha Shivratri holds immense spiritual and religious importance in Hinduism. It is considered the most auspicious day to worship Lord Shiva and seek his divine blessings. Devotees believe that sincere prayers, fasting, and worship on this day remove negative karma, forgive past sins, and bring inner peace, prosperity, and spiritual growth.

The festival also celebrates the divine union of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati, symbolizing harmony, devotion, and the balance of masculine and feminine energies. Many devotees perform Rudrabhishek and chant the Maha Mrityunjaya Mantra and Om Namah Shivaya to receive Lord Shiva’s grace and protection.

Puja Vidhi

1. Wake up early, take a holy bath, and observe a fast with devotion.
2. Clean the puja area and place or visit a Shiva Linga.
3. Perform Abhishek with water, milk, honey, curd, ghee, sugar, and Gangajal.
4. Offer Bel Patra, Datura, white flowers, fruits, and Bhasma to Lord Shiva.
5. Chant Om Namah Shivaya and the Maha Mrityunjaya Mantra.
6. Recite the Shiva Chalisa or Shiva Purana.
7. Perform Shiva Aarti and stay awake during the night (Jagaran) with bhajans and prayers.
8. Break the fast the next morning according to family tradition.

Related Katha / Mantra / Aarti / Chalisa

* Maha Shivratri Vrat Katha
* Shiva Mantra
* Maha Mrityunjaya Mantra
* Shiva Aarti
* Shiva Chalisa
* Rudrashtakam
* Shiva Ashtakam
* Shiva Purana

faq.a1: Maha Shivratri is celebrated on the Chaturdashi of the Krishna Paksha in the Hindu month of Phalguna.
faq.a2: It is celebrated to honor Lord Shiva and commemorate events such as his divine marriage with Goddess Parvati and the appearance of the infinite Shiva Linga.
faq.q3: Who is worshipped on Maha Shivratri?
faq.a3: Lord Shiva is worshipped on Maha Shivratri.
faq.q4: In which month is Maha Shivratri celebrated?
faq.a4: Maha Shivratri usually falls in February or March.
faq.q5: What is offered to Lord Shiva on Maha Shivratri?
faq.a5: Milk, water, Gangajal, honey, Bel Patra, Datura, fruits, and white flowers are commonly offered.
faq.q6: Which mantra is chanted on Maha Shivratri?
faq.a6: The Om Namah Shivaya and Maha Mrityunjaya Mantra are the most commonly chanted mantras.
faq.q7: Why do devotees observe a fast on Maha Shivratri?
faq.a7: Devotees observe the fast to seek Lord Shiva’s blessings for health, prosperity, spiritual growth, and liberation (Moksha).
faq.q8: What is the spiritual significance of Maha Shivratri?
faq.a8: Maha Shivratri symbolizes devotion, self-discipline, spiritual awakening, and the victory of light, wisdom, and righteousness over ignorance.
